text.title: mTLS Service-to-Service Deployment Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the cicd-mtls-deployment methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale and is referenced by the decision tree.

rule.statement: mTLS MUST use an internal CA (step-ca, Vault PKI, service-mesh issuer) for service identities; public CAs (Let's Encrypt) MUST NOT be used for service-to-service auth.
rule.rationale: Public CAs issue identities trusted by the world; internal CAs restrict the trust domain to your services.

rule.statement: Per-pod / per-service certs MUST have lifetime ≤ 24h; long-lived client certs (>30 days) are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Long-lived certs amplify revocation blast radius; short-lived certs auto-expire on compromise.

rule.statement: Each workload identity MUST have its own cert; sharing one client cert across N services is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Shared certs make impersonation undetectable and rotation impossible without coordinated downtime.

rule.statement: nginx / envoy MUST be configured with `ssl_verify_client on` (or envoy equivalent) and require a non-empty `$ssl_client_s_dn` to forward requests.
rule.rationale: Without `ssl_verify_client on`, mTLS terminates without enforcement and any client cert is accepted.

rule.statement: Either CRL distribution point OR OCSP stapling MUST be configured to revoke compromised certs before they expire naturally.
rule.rationale: Without revocation, a stolen private key remains valid until the (short) cert expires; CRL/OCSP shrinks the exposure window.

rule.statement: If application-layer auth (OIDC/JWT-PoP) already provides cryptographic service-to-service auth equivalent to mTLS, this methodology MUST be skipped.
rule.rationale: Layering mTLS on top of equivalent app-layer auth duplicates infra without security gain.
